rows.title: CollidersAndNon
rows.tips:

rows.addition_to_the_LN:
\textbf{Addition tag \texttt{[collider\_side\_aware\_walkstep\_predicates]}.}
This addition refines the literal LN definition by introducing a \emph{side-aware}
reading of the per-walk-step contribution to $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ at the two adjacent
walk-positions $k \in \{i, i+1\}$ of a walk-step $a_i$ of $\pi$. The refinement is
authorised because the literal LN definition is ambiguous in two corner configurations:
(i) at a walk-incident directed self-loop $a_i \in E$ with $v_i = v_{i+1}$, where both
walk-constraint disjuncts of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.\ apply syntactically and the
stored-pair test of def \ref{def-edge-relations}, item~ii.\ fires unconditionally at
both adjacent positions; and (ii) at a walk-step traversing a pair that
simultaneously sits in $E$ as an ordered pair and in $L$ as an unordered pair -- the
\emph{writing-mirror} configuration permitted by def \ref{def-cdmg} (which does not
impose $E \cap L = \emptyset$) -- where the literal stored-pair test's L-disjunct fires
at both adjacent positions regardless of which channel the walk-step actually traverses.
The side-aware reading introduced here resolves both corners by committing to the
walk-step's recorded traversal channel as the \emph{sole} signal for which channel
contributes to the arrowhead test at this step, refining (and strictly overriding) the
literal stored-pair test as the canonical reading of $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ used by the
collider/non-collider classification.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{(a) Per-step walk-traversal channel and direction are recorded
explicitly.} Each walk-step $a_i$ of $\pi$ carries an explicit walk-traversal channel
$c_i \in \{\textsf{forward-}E, \textsf{backward-}E, \textsf{bidir-}L\}$, with the
convention
\[
    c_i = \textsf{forward-}E \iff \bigl(a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \;\land\; a_i \in E\bigr),
\]
\[
    c_i = \textsf{backward-}E \iff \bigl(a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \;\land\; a_i \in E\bigr),
\]
\[
    c_i = \textsf{bidir-}L \iff a_i \in L \;\text{(taken with}\; a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1})
    \;\text{by convention)}.
\]
At a walk-step whose endpoints are distinct and that does not sit in a writing-mirror
pair, the channel $c_i$ is forced by the data of $\pi$. At a writing-mirror walk-step
($(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$ \emph{and} $s(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in L$, with $v_i \ne v_{i+1}$
or $v_i = v_{i+1}$), the choice of $c_i \in \{\textsf{forward-}E, \textsf{backward-}E,
\textsf{bidir-}L\}$ is \emph{recorded explicitly} as part of $\pi$ -- i.e.\ $\pi$
carries strictly more data at such steps than its underlying vertex/edge sequence alone.
At a walk-incident directed self-loop $a_i \in E$ with $v_i = v_{i+1}$, the choice
between $c_i = \textsf{forward-}E$ and $c_i = \textsf{backward-}E$ is similarly
recorded explicitly.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{(b) Side-aware per-step contribution formula at adjacent positions.}
For each walk-step $a_i$ of $\pi$ ($0 \le i \le n - 1$) and each adjacent position
$k \in \{i, i+1\}$, the contribution of $a_i$ to $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ is determined
\emph{solely} by the recorded channel $c_i$, never consulting whether the underlying
stored pair also sits in the opposite channel:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Forward-$E$ step} ($c_i = \textsf{forward-}E$): contributes $1$ at the
        walk-traversal target $k = i + 1$ and $0$ at the walk-traversal source $k = i$.
    \item \emph{Backward-$E$ step} ($c_i = \textsf{backward-}E$): contributes $1$ at
        the walk-traversal target $k = i$ and $0$ at the walk-traversal source $k = i + 1$.
    \item \emph{Bidir-$L$ step} ($c_i = \textsf{bidir-}L$): contributes $1$ at both
        adjacent positions $k \in \{i, i+1\}$.
\end{itemize}
A directed step (forward-$E$ or backward-$E$) commits to its $E$-side traversal even
when a coexisting $L$-edge $s(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in L$ would, under the literal stored-pair
test, contribute additional arrowheads at the source position. A bidirected step
($\textsf{bidir-}L$) commits to the $L$-side bidirected contribution even when a
coexisting $E$-edge would, under the literal test, contribute differently. The
arrowhead count $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ at a position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$ is then
the sum of these per-step contributions, summed over the (at most two) walk-incident
steps $i \in \{k - 1, k\} \cap \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{(c) Deviation from the literal stored-pair test is strict at
writing-mirror walk-steps and at directed self-loops.}
The side-aware contribution formula of item~(b) coincides pointwise with the literal
stored-pair ``$a_i$ is an edge into $v_k$'' test of def \ref{def-edge-relations},
item~ii.\ on any walk $\pi$ that traverses neither a writing-mirror configuration nor
a directed self-loop. Where the two readings disagree:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Directed self-loop steps} ($a_i \in E$ with $v_i = v_{i+1}$, no
        coexisting $L$-edge): the literal test fires unconditionally at both adjacent
        positions, contributing $1 + 1 = 2$; the side-aware reading commits via $c_i$
        to a single position (the walk-traversal target) for the contribution,
        contributing $1$ at that position and $0$ at the other.
    \item \emph{Writing-mirror walk-steps} ($(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$ \emph{and}
        $s(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in L$, with $v_i \ne v_{i+1}$): the literal test fires the
        L-disjunct at both adjacent positions regardless of which channel the
        walk-step traverses; the side-aware reading ignores the coexisting $L$-edge
        when $c_i \in \{\textsf{forward-}E, \textsf{backward-}E\}$, contributing only
        at the walk-traversal target of the directed channel.
    \item \emph{Writing-mirror self-loop steps} ($(v, v) \in E$ \emph{and}
        $s(v, v) \in L$): structurally impossible by def \ref{def-cdmg}'s
        \texttt{hL\_irrefl}, which forbids self-pair elements in $L$.
\end{itemize}
The strict-equivalence gate accepts this deviation as the canonical disambiguation of
the LN's ``$\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ counts arrowheads on the walk $\pi$'' phrasing -- a
walk-step \emph{is} the traversal of a single channel of the graph, and an arrowhead
that the walk's channel does not place at $v_k$ is not part of the walk's count, even
when the same pair sits in the opposite channel of the graph as a separate edge object.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Canonical classification.}
The collider / non-collider classification of def \ref{def:collider_noncollider} is
defined using the side-aware $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ of items~(a)--(b): position
$k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ iff its side-aware arrowhead
count satisfies $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$, and a collider iff $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = 2$.
The side-aware reading is the canonical disambiguation of the LN's ``i.e.''\ gloss
``at most one arrowhead'' (resp.\ ``two arrowheads pointing towards $v_k$ on the walk
$\pi$''), taking precedence over the literal stored-pair test at both corner kinds
listed in item~(c). On walks avoiding both corners, the classification coincides with
the count obtained from the literal stored-pair test.

wording_check.output:
I have the full context now. Let me consolidate my findings.

Key insights from `not-cdmg` (def_3_2) and `def-cdmg` (def_3_1):
- CDMG edges are `\tuh` (→), `\hut` (←), `\huh` (↔) — exactly three types.
- "Star" (`s`) is a placeholder explicitly meaning "arrowhead or tail" (line 67).
- `\suh` = `\tuh` OR `\huh` (arrowhead at right).
- `\hus` = `\hut` OR `\huh` (arrowhead at left).
- Trivial walk (n=0) is allowed (line 104).

So the non-collider enumeration IS exhaustive for the {→, ←, ↔} edge set: every middle position has 4 possible (arrowhead-at-v_k from left, arrowhead-at-v_k from right) combinations — fork (t,t), left chain (t,h), right chain (h,t), collider (h,h). And end-nodes only have ≤1 walk-edge so the "end-node" case covers them trivially.

The definition is internally consistent for the CDMG edge set. However, there are a few literal-reading subtleties a formalizer should know.

```
SUBTLETY:
id: bidirected_edges_included_in_left_and_right_chain
explanation: The "left chain" case is written as `v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1}` and the "right chain" as `v_{k-1} \suh v_k \tuh v_{k+1}`. By def_3_2 (not-cdmg), `\hus` expands to `\hut` OR `\huh`, and `\suh` expands to `\tuh` OR `\huh`. So left chain admits BOTH `v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hut v_{k+1}` (classical chain v_{k-1} ← v_k ← v_{k+1}) AND `v_{k-1} \hut v_k \huh v_{k+1}` (the mixed configuration v_{k-1} ← v_k ↔ v_{k+1}, where the right edge is bidirected). Right chain similarly admits v_{k-1} ↔ v_k → v_{k+1}. A reader expecting "chain" to mean a pure directed sequence (← ← or → →) would miss these bidirected-inclusive cases. Concrete corner case: a position with v_{k-1} ↔ v_k → v_{k+1} has exactly one arrowhead pointing into v_k (from the left bidirected edge), so by the verbal criterion "at most one arrowhead" it is a non-collider, and the LN classifies it under "right chain" via the wildcard `\suh`. A formalizer translating "right chain" naively as v_{k-1} → v_k → v_{k+1} would under-cover the non-collider cases, leaving the bidirected-mixed case unclassified — which would then leak as a wrong/missing classification in downstream σ-blocking / σ-separation arguments.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: collider_term_only_defined_for_middle_positions
explanation: The collider clause says "if it is of the form: $v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1}$, i.e.\ if there are two arrowheads pointing towards $v_k$ on the walk $\pi$." This literal form mentions both $v_{k-1}$ and $v_{k+1}$, so it tacitly assumes 0 < k < n (middle position). For end-nodes (k=0 or k=n) neither $v_{k-1}$ nor $v_{k+1}$ exists on the relevant side, so the collider form simply does not apply, and the end-node falls under non-collider by the explicit "end-node: k ∈ {0,n}" clause. This is consistent and exclusive — every position is exactly one of collider/non-collider — but the LN never spells out the "middle position" precondition for the collider clause. A literal reader trying to evaluate "is the form $v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1}$ satisfied?" at k=0 would have to decide whether the missing $v_{-1}$ makes the form "false" (so non-collider) or "undefined" (so undefined). The intended reading is the former, but it's not stated. Particularly worth flagging because for the trivial walk (n=0, just $v_0$), the only position is k=0=n, which is forced into the end-node non-collider case purely by the v_{k-1}/v_{k+1} arguments not existing.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: end_node_non_collider_with_arrowhead_pointing_in
explanation: The end-node case classifies position k ∈ {0,n} as a non-collider unconditionally, regardless of the mark on its single adjacent walk-edge. Concretely, an end-node v_0 with `v_0 \hus v_1` (i.e., v_0 ← v_1 or v_0 ↔ v_1, both placing an arrowhead at v_0) is classified as a non-collider, even though there is 1 arrowhead "pointing towards" v_0 on the walk. This matches the verbal criterion "at most one arrowhead" (1 ≤ 1), but it can be counter-intuitive: a reader (or downstream proof) tempted to characterize colliders as "any position with an arrowhead pointing into it on the walk" would misclassify such end-nodes. This is especially relevant for arguments about walk endpoints — e.g., a walk that ends with v_{n-1} ↔ v_n: position n has an incoming arrowhead from the walk yet is non-collider by definition. Downstream σ-blocking definitions typically only require descendants-of-collider for the *interior* positions of a walk, consistent with this convention, but the asymmetry between the end-node case (mark-independent) and the middle cases (mark-dependent) is worth pinning down explicitly when formalizing.
END_SUBTLETY
```

rows.title: BlockableAndUnblockable
rows.tips:

wording_check.output:
Now I have full context. The CDMG has edges $E$ (directed) and $L$ (bidirected — symmetric, two heads). The shorthand symbols (`\hut`, `\hus`, etc.) per def_3_2 are EXISTENCE claims about edges in $G$, not statements about the walk's specific edges. And def_3_15 explicitly reconciled this ambiguity for the collider/non-collider classification, but def_3_16 does not.

Let me write my audit.

SUBTLETY:
id: pattern_shorthands_existential_in_g_not_walk_specific
explanation: The unblockable patterns "$v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1}$", "$v_{k-1} \suh v_k \tuh v_{k+1}$", and "$v_{k-1} \hut v_k \tuh v_{k+1}$" use shorthand defined in def_3_2 as *existence* claims about edges in $G$ (e.g. $v_{k-1} \hut v_k :\iff (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$). The verbal characterization that precedes the patterns — "it only has outgoing edges **on $\pi$** to nodes in the same strongly connected component of $G$" — is about the walk's *specific* incident edges $a_{k-1}, a_k$.

Def_3_15 had to explicitly reconcile this exact mismatch ("the LN writing $v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1}$ ... spelled out via the walk constraint, $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$..."), declaring the count-based, walk-specific reading "canonical". Def_3_16 inherits the shorthand without restating this reconciliation.

Concrete corner case: suppose between adjacent walk-positions $v_{k-1}$ and $v_k$ the graph contains *both* $(v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$ and $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$, and the walk uses $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1})$. Suppose further $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$, so on the walk $v_k$ is a fork (count $= 0$, both walk-incident edges out of $v_k$). Now suppose only $v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ while $v_{k+1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$. Walk-based reading: $v_k$ has an outgoing walk-edge ($a_k$) to a different SC component, so it should be blockable. But existential pattern reading: $v_{k-1} \hut v_k$ holds (one of the $E$-edges supplies it) and $v_k \hus v_{k+1}$ requires $(v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$ or $(v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$. If either of those exists (e.g. add a bidirected edge $(v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$ to the example), the "left chain" pattern matches existentially and the condition $v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ is met — so the literal pattern classification calls $v_k$ unblockable, contradicting the walk-based intent.

Downstream this matters for any proof about $\sigma$-blocking (def_3_17 cites this definition): a formalization that follows the patterns literally will disagree with one that follows the verbal "outgoing edges on $\pi$" characterization, exactly on positions whose walk-incident edges differ from other edges that the shorthand existence-quantifies over.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: self_loop_pattern_overlap_inherited
explanation: Def_3_15 explicitly warns that at a directed self-loop traversed by a walk — i.e. at a position $k$ with $v_{k-1} = v_k$ via $(v,v) \in E$ — "a single interior position $k$ traversing a directed self-loop can simultaneously satisfy the 'left chain', 'right chain', 'fork', and 'collider' pattern conjunctions" under the literal def_3_2 reading of $\hut, \tuh, \hus, \suh$. Def_3_16 reuses these same patterns ("left chain", "right chain", "fork") without restating this caveat, so the same overlap occurs here. Concretely: if $v_{k-1} = v_k = v$ with $(v,v) \in E$, then $v_{k-1} \hut v_k$ and $v_k \tuh v_{k-1}$ collapse to the same condition $(v,v) \in E$, and $v \in \Sc^G(v)$ trivially, so the "fork" unblockable pattern matches automatically — even though the walk's actual classification (per def_3_15's canonical count-based reading) might be collider. Whether $v_k$ ends up "unblockable non-collider" thus depends on which reading governs def_3_16; the text doesn't pick one explicitly.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: blockable_clause_says_arrow_not_outgoing_edge
explanation: The "blockable" elaboration says "it has at least one outgoing arrow $v_k \tuh v_{k\pm 1}$ pointing to a node $v_{k\pm 1}$ that lies in a different strongly connected component". Per def_3_2 item 2, $v_k \tuh v_{k\pm 1}$ is the *existence* of $(v_k, v_{k\pm 1}) \in E$ in $G$, regardless of whether that directed edge is the walk's specific incident edge at position $k$.

So under the literal reading: $v_k$ can have an outgoing arrow $v_k \tuh v_{k-1}$ in $G$ to a node $v_{k-1} \notin \Sc^G(v_k)$ even if the walk's $a_{k-1}$ is actually $(v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$ (into $v_k$). The blockable condition would then literally be satisfied via an edge that is not the walk's edge. Combined with the symmetric ambiguity in the unblockable patterns, $v_k$ could literally satisfy both "unblockable left chain" (via some auxiliary edge configuration meeting same-SC) and "blockable via outgoing arrow to non-SC" (via the auxiliary $v_k \tuh v_{k-1}$ to non-SC $v_{k-1}$). The intent is clearly the walk-edge reading, but the text says "outgoing arrow $v_k \tuh v_{k\pm 1}$", not "the walk's $a_{k\pm 1}$ is an outgoing edge from $v_k$ to a node not in $\Sc^G(v_k)$".
END_SUBTLETY

rows.title: AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable
rows.tips:
Refactored on 2026-06-15 via `blockable_noncollider_first` (see leanification/Chapter*/Refactor_blockable_noncollider_first_DONE_*/refactor_data.json).

Refactored on 2026-06-19 via `cdmg_typed_edges` (see leanification/Chapter*/Refactor_cdmg_typed_edges_DONE_*/refactor_data.json).

rows.addition_to_the_LN: [acyclic_does_not_imply_directed_in_text] In this remark $G$ is a CDMG, as fixed by the surrounding context; the hypothesis "$G$ is acyclic" is to be read as acyclicity in the CDMG sense, so the edge orientations needed to define (non-)colliders are already part of the ambient structure.

wording_check.output:
Based on my analysis, let me audit this claim carefully.

The claim states: "If $G$ is acyclic then all non-colliders are blockable."

Let me verify by checking the relevant definitions:
- **def_3_15** (CollidersAndNon): non-collider is per-walk (end-node, left chain, right chain, or fork pattern)
- **def_3_16** (BlockableAndUnblockable): unblockable requires non-end-node AND all directed outgoing edges go to $\Sc^G(v_k)$; otherwise blockable
- **def_3_6** (Acyclicity): no non-trivial directed walk from $v$ to itself
- **$\Sc^G(v)$**: $\Anc^G(v) \cap \Desc^G(v)$, always contains $v$

In an acyclic CDMG, $\Sc^G(v_k) = \{v_k\}$ (otherwise we'd have a non-trivial directed cycle). For an unblockable non-collider, the outgoing edge $v_k \tuh v_{k\pm1}$ would require $v_{k\pm1} \in \{v_k\}$, i.e., a self-loop — but self-loops are excluded by acyclicity (length-1 directed walk from $v_k$ to itself). End-nodes are trivially blockable. So the claim holds straightforwardly.

The wording uses standard mathematical shorthand: "all non-colliders are blockable" implicitly quantifies over walks and positions (since both notions are per-walk). This is unambiguous given the surrounding definitions.

The edge cases all behave reasonably:
- Walks of length 0/1: end-nodes only, trivially blockable
- Empty graphs / no walks: vacuously true
- Self-loops: excluded by acyclicity

```
NO_SUBTLETIES
```

rows.title: SigmaBlockedWalks

The original declaration and tex block live at the same `main_lean_file` / `tex_file` paths as this row points at -- read them first as inspiration. Use the same-file marker convention from manager.md (## Refactor rows) to add the replacement alongside the original; the cleanup script will swap them atomically at the end of the table.
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [claim_type_mismatch_vertex_vs_walk] The trailing claim asserting that unblockable non-colliders are always $C$-$\sigma$-open is excluded from the formalization. It is informal commentary, not part of Definition~\ref{def:sigma_blocking}, and no Lean obligation should be derived from it.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: trailing_claim_applies_sigma_open_to_vertices_not_walks
explanation: The formal definition introduces "C-σ-open" exclusively as a property of WALKS: "We say that the walk π is: ... C-σ-open (or σ-open given C) if and only if [conditions on colliders and blockable non-colliders along π]". However, the embedded claim at the end states:

"Note that unblockable non-colliders are always $C$-$\sigma$-open, regardless of the subset $C \ins V \cup J$."

This applies the predicate "C-σ-open" to *vertices* (specifically, unblockable non-collider occurrences on a walk), not to walks. The formal definition gives no meaning to "a non-collider is C-σ-open" — only walks have been declared open or blocked. So the literal reading is a category/type mismatch with the just-stated definition.

Two plausible intended readings:
(a) "An unblockable non-collider on a walk does not contribute to blocking" — i.e., condition (ii) of σ-blocked only quantifies over BLOCKABLE non-colliders, so unblockable ones are silently ignored. This is the safest reading and is essentially a remark about which vertices appear in condition (ii).
(b) An implicit per-vertex extension of σ-open: a non-collider v_k is σ-open given C iff v_k is unblockable, or (v_k is blockable and v_k ∉ C). Under this reading the walk-level σ-open would decompose as "every collider is in Anc(C) and every non-collider is vertex-level σ-open."

Neither reading is made explicit. A downstream proof or formalization that wants to invoke this remark (e.g., to argue case-by-case along the walk that no individual non-collider blocks, when proving σ-openness of a constructed walk) will have to commit to interpretation (a) or (b). If a future definition or claim writes phrases like "v_k is σ-open" expecting the vertex-level extension (b), this remark is the only textual hook for that reading — and it is not actually a definition.

A secondary, smaller note: the opening line writes "$C \ins J \cup V$" while the trailing claim writes "$C \ins V \cup J$". Same set as a union, but the swapped order is a minor cosmetic inconsistency.
END_SUBTLETY

Use the same-file marker convention from manager.md (## Refactor rows) to add the replacement alongside the original; the cleanup script will swap them atomically at the end of the table.
rows.addition_to_the_LN: [sigma_symmetry_claim_invokes_unstated_reversal_invariance] In a CDMG, a walk is a sequence of incident edges without regard to edge orientation; consequently, every walk from $A$ to $B$ is, read in reverse, a walk from $B$ to $A$, and this reversal is an involution on the set of walks. The symmetry of $\sigma$-separation (when $J = \emptyset$) is to be understood as resting on this walk-reversal involution together with the fact that the $\sigma$-blocking conditions on internal nodes are stated in a manner invariant under reversal.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: empty_b_non_vacuous_when_j_nonempty
explanation: The literal definition of $A \isPerp_G B \given C$ requires "every walk from a node in $A$ to a node in $J \cup B$" to be $\sigma$-blocked. Consider the corner case $B = \emptyset$ with $J \neq \emptyset$. One might naively expect "separation from the empty set" to be vacuously true (standard separoid intuition: $A \perp \emptyset \given C$ holds trivially because there's nothing to separate from). But here $J \cup B = J \cup \emptyset = J$ is nonempty, so $A \isPerp_G \emptyset \given C$ literally asserts that every walk from $A$ into the input nodes $J$ is $\sigma$-blocked — a non-trivial condition, not a vacuous truth. This is a direct consequence of the (sic!) choice to include $J$ in the target, but the footnote justifies it only via separoid-rules-matching-Markov-kernels, not by explicitly flagging this loss of the usual vacuity. A reader who sees "$A \isPerp_G \emptyset \given C$" downstream and reaches for vacuity would be wrong whenever $J \neq \emptyset$.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: symmetry_claim_walks_between_wording_imprecise
explanation: The embedded claim (marked `% claim_3_22`) justifies $\sigma$-separation symmetry by saying "when $J = \emptyset$ the set of walks between $A$ and $B$ is the same regardless of direction". Read literally, this is imprecise: walks in a CDMG are directed sequences of vertices and edges, so the *set* of walks from $A$ to $B$ is not literally equal to the *set* of walks from $B$ to $A$. What is true is that walk *reversal* induces a bijection between these two sets, and the $\sigma$-blocking property is invariant under that bijection (because the collider/non-collider role of each internal vertex is preserved by reversal). The current wording conflates "set equality" with "bijection-preserving-blocking-status", which weakens the proof sketch: a careful reader trying to formalize this claim from the literal text would have to recover the missing reversal-bijection step themselves. This matters if claim_3_22 is later proved from this justification.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: overlap_with_j_or_target_creates_self_walks
explanation: The definition explicitly permits $A, B, C \ins J \cup V$ "not necessarily disjoint". Combined with the asymmetric inclusion of $J$ on the right ("walks from a node in $A$ to a node in $J \cup B$"), this creates a literal-reading corner case: if $A \cap (J \cup B) \neq \emptyset$ — in particular if $A \cap J \neq \emptyset$, or if $A \cap B \neq \emptyset$, or even if $A = B$ — then every node $a \in A \cap (J \cup B)$ is simultaneously a starting node *and* an endpoint of the "walk from a node in $A$ to a node in $J \cup B$" quantification. Whether this forces trivial/length-zero walks (e.g. the walk $\langle a \rangle$) to be required to be $\sigma$-blocked depends entirely on whether the upstream walk definition admits length-zero walks (a question this block leaves implicit). If it does, then e.g. $A \isPerp_G A \given C$ degenerates to a condition about every $a \in A$ having its trivial self-walk $\sigma$-blocked by $C$, which is a very different statement than the usual "separation of $A$ from itself". The tex block does not pin this down; downstream lemmas like "$A \isPerp_G B$ when $A = B \subseteq C$" could hinge on this convention.
END_SUBTLETY

rows.title: SigmaSeparationSymmetric
rows.tips:
OPERATOR HINT (2026-06-16) — walk-reversal strategy for claim_3_22

The previous solve attempt built a generic walk-reversal helper (`reverseAuxEdge` in a now-discarded local file) that swaps the stored ordered pair for L-edges to satisfy `WalkStep`'s forward-L disjunct via `hL_symm`. That swap creates a 'writing-mirror' artifact: when `(u, v) ∈ G.L` and `(v, u) ∈ G.E` coexist (admitted by the current `def_3_1` per its `[edge_set_disjointness_under_specified]` clarification), the swapped pair coincidentally lands in `G.E` and confuses the literal-pair pattern in `def_3_16`'s `IsBlockableNonCollider` — making the predicate's verdict at the interior vertex differ between the forward and reversed walks. The previous attempt escalated to a `def_3_1` refactor; the operator declined.

STRATEGY: define a CUSTOM LOCAL walk-reversal helper (or proof technique) for THIS row's proof that preserves the channel information across reversal — without changing `def_3_4` (Walk / WalkStep), `def_3_16` (blockable predicate), or `def_3_1` (CDMG). Possible local approaches:

  (i) Define a custom reversed-walk type alongside `Walk` that tags each step's original channel (E forward / E backward / L). Reverse against the tagged type; the tag survives swap. Predicates destructure the tag directly, no inference from stored pair.

  (ii) Avoid constructing the full reversed `Walk` object. Prove the per-position equivalence `p.IsBlockableNonCollider k ↔ p.reverse.IsBlockableNonCollider (p.length - k)` directly by case-analysing on the original walk's `WalkStep` disjunct (which is propositionally available on the original walk), bypassing the channel-inference-from-stored-pair step entirely.

MATH-LEVEL OBSERVATION: under J = ∅, σ-separation symmetry is trivially true. Every walk's classification at each interior vertex depends only on the EDGES of `G` incident at that vertex (through the LN's 'outgoing E-arrowhead at v_k' notion), not on the walk's traversal direction. The Lean proof should reflect that direction-invariance more or less directly, not through a heavy reversal-construction-and-prove-it-preserves-everything pipeline. If you find yourself spinning up >500 lines of generic walk-reversal infrastructure, you are probably on the wrong path.

If after a genuine attempt at (i) or (ii) the local approach doesn't pan out, `refactor` is still available (the one-time nudge gate will let you re-emit after one local-fix exploration turn) — but please articulate concretely why each local approach fails in the refactor body.

REFACTOR row. This row is a DEPENDENT in refactor `collider_side_aware`; it was pulled in because root(s) `def_3_15` changed underneath it. The original declaration and tex block live at the same `main_lean_file` / `tex_file` paths as this row points at -- read them first as inspiration. Use the same-file marker convention from manager.md (## Refactor rows) to add the replacement alongside the original; the cleanup script will swap them atomically at the end of the table.

wording_check.output:
I have full context now. Let me identify subtleties in the claim's wording.

```
SUBTLETY:
id: walks_between_a_b_directionality_ambiguous
explanation: The justification states "the set of walks between $A$ and $B$ is the same regardless of direction," but this conflicts with how walks are defined and used elsewhere in the very same section. Walks in def_3_17 (and def_3_15) are directed sequences $\pi = (v_0 \sus \cdots \sus v_n)$ with a designated start $v_0$ and end $v_n$. Moreover, def_3_18 ($i\sigma$-separation, which $\sigma$-separation specializes) speaks of "every walk from a node in $A$ to a node in $J \cup B$" — i.e., walks from $A$ to $B$ are directional: $v_0 \in A$, $v_n \in B$. Under this definitional reading, the set of walks from $A$ to $B$ is generally NOT literally the same as the set of walks from $B$ to $A$; they are merely in bijection via the reversal map $\pi \mapsto (v_n \sus \cdots \sus v_0)$. The literal reading "same set regardless of direction" is therefore incorrect unless one silently re-interprets "walks between $A$ and $B$" to mean either (i) walks where one endpoint lies in $A$ and the other in $B$ (a symmetric notion) or (ii) walks up to reversal. The proof of symmetry actually requires constructing/invoking this reversal bijection and showing it maps walks-from-$A$-to-$B$ bijectively to walks-from-$B$-to-$A$ — the claim's wording elides this step.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: sigma_blocking_reversal_invariance_unjustified
explanation: The second half of the justification asserts that "the $\sigma$-blocking conditions are invariant under walk reversal" as if this were immediate. It is true, but it rests on several non-trivial structural facts that the claim does not surface: (a) the collider definition in def_3_15 ($v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1}$) is symmetric in the two adjacent edges, so reversing the walk preserves which positions are colliders; (b) the (un)blockable non-collider definition in def_3_16 — which involves outgoing edges to the same SCC — is similarly symmetric under reversal, because "outgoing" is a property of the underlying edge orientation in $G$, not of the walk's traversal direction; (c) membership in $\Anc^G(C)$ and in $C$ does not depend on the walk at all. Without naming these facts, a reader (or a formalization) cannot tell whether "invariant under walk reversal" is a definitional triviality or a lemma requiring its own proof. The literal claim is, in effect, the entire content of the symmetry proof packaged into one unjustified phrase.
END_SUBTLETY
```

rows.title: LabelRoman
rows.tips: REFACTOR row. This row is a DEPENDENT in refactor `collider_side_aware`; it was pulled in because root(s) `def_3_15` changed underneath it. The original declaration and tex block live at the same `main_lean_file` / `tex_file` paths as this row points at -- read them first as inspiration. Use the same-file marker convention from manager.md (## Refactor rows) to add the replacement alongside the original; the cleanup script will swap them atomically at the end of the table.
rows.tex_block:
\begin{claimmark}
\begin{Lem}\label{lem:replace_walk}
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG, $C \subseteq V \cup J$ and $\pi = \lp  v_0 \sus \cdots \sus v_n \rp$ be a $C$-$\sigma$-open walk in $G$.
  Suppose $v_i \in \Sc^G(v_j)$ for some $i,j \in \{0,\dots,n\}$ with $i < j$.
If we then replace the subwalk $v_i \sus \cdots \sus v_j$ of $\pi$ by 
  \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
      \item a shortest directed path $v_i \tuh \cdots \tuh v_j$ in $G$ if $j = n$ or if $v_j \tuh v_{j+1}$ on $\pi$, or 
    \item a shortest directed path $v_i \hut \cdots \hut v_j$ in $G$ otherwise,
  \end{enumerate}
  then this new subwalk is entirely within $\Sc^G(v_j)$ and the modified walk $\pi'$ is still $C$-$\sigma$-open.
\begin{proof}
$\pi'$ cannot become $C$-$\sigma$-blocked at one of the initial nodes $v_0, \dots, v_{i-1}$ or at one of the final nodes $v_{j+1}, \dots, v_n$ on $\pi'$, since these nodes occur in the same local configuration on $\pi$ and are not $C$-$\sigma$-blocked on $\pi$ by assumption.
Furthermore, $\pi'$ cannot become $C$-$\sigma$-blocked at one of the nodes strictly between $v_i$ and $v_j$ on $\pi'$ (if there are any), since these nodes are all non-endnode non-colliders that only point to nodes in the same strongly connected component $\Sc^G(v_j)$. It is also worth noting that $\pi'$ cannot become $C$-$\sigma$-blocked at any of its endnodes, which could be $v_i$ or $v_j$ or both, because those are the same in $\pi$. So in the following we can w.l.o.g.\ assume that both $v_i$ and $v_j$ are non-endnodes of $\pi$ and thus $\pi'$.

Case (i). By assumption $v_j$ is either a fork or a right chain (or the right endnode) on $\pi$ that is $C$-$\sigma$-open. Since the same blocking criteria apply to $v_j$ on $\pi'$ it remains $C$-$\sigma$-open on $\pi'$. If $v_i=v_j$ then also $v_i$ is $C$-$\sigma$-open on $\pi'$ (if $v_i$ is the left endnode or not). If $v_i \neq v_j$, then the new directed path $v_i \tuh \cdots \tuh v_j$ in $\pi'$ is $C$-$\sigma$-open at $v_i$ because all nodes in between lie in the same stronly connected component $\Sc^G(v_i)$ (or $v_i$ is the left endnode anyways).

 Case (ii). Since case (i) is solved we can assume that we have $j<n$ with $v_j \hus v_{j+1}$ in $\pi$. 
 If $v_{i-1} \hut v_i$ on $\pi'$ (or $v_i$ the left endnode) then this case is analogous to case (i). 
 So we can also assume that we have $i > 0$ and $v_{i-1} \suh v_i$ on $\pi$. So $\pi$ looks as follows:
 \[\pi:\qquad \cdots v_{i-1} \suh v_i \sus \cdots \sus v_j \hus v_{j+1} \cdots. \]
 So there must be a smallest number $k \in \lC i,\dots,j\rC$ such that a collider appears at $v_k$ on $\pi$:
 \[\pi:\qquad \cdots v_{i-1} \suh v_i \tuh \cdots \tuh v_k \hus \cdots \sus v_j \hus v_{j+1} \cdots. \]
 Since $\pi$ is $C$-$\sigma$-open we have $v_k \in \Anc^G(C)$. Since $v_i \in \Anc^G(v_k)$ (otherwise $v_k$ would not be the first collider appearing after $v_i$) we thus have that also $v_i \in \Anc^G(C)$.
 So if we replace the subwalk $v_i \sus \cdots \sus v_j$ of $\pi$ by the shortest directed path $v_i \hut \cdots \hut v_j$ in $G$ we then get for $\pi'$ the following situation:
 \[\pi':\qquad \cdots v_{i-1} \suh v_i \hut \cdots \hut  v_j \hus v_{j+1} \cdots, \]
 which is then $C$-$\sigma$-open at $v_i$ as $v_i \in \Anc^G(C)$. Note that this holds also when $v_i=v_j$.
 If $v_i \neq v_j$ then $v_j$ is also $C$-$\sigma$-open on $\pi'$ as $v_j$ points left to a node in the same strongly connected component as $v_j$. 

 So in all cases $\pi'$ stays $C$-$\sigma$-open.
\end{proof}
\end{Lem}
\end{claimmark}
